options.text: Decrease the watermark duration on the stateful aggregation to clear the state store
options.explanation: Changing the watermark duration alters how long state is retained going forward; it does not resolve a corrupted or incompatible state store from a prior pipeline version.

options.text: Delete the pipeline and recreate it with a new pipeline ID to reset the checkpoint
options.explanation: Deleting and recreating the pipeline is unnecessarily destructive; the full refresh option on the existing pipeline achieves the same reset without losing the pipeline definition and configuration.

questions.stem: A bundle deployment to staging succeeds but the same bundle fails in production. Select TWO investigation steps that are MOST likely to identify environment-specific configuration differences. (Select TWO)

options.text: Run `databricks bundle validate --target prod` to see the fully-rendered production configuration including variable substitutions
options.explanation: `databricks bundle validate --target prod` renders the final YAML with all environment-specific variable substitutions applied, making it easy to spot configuration differences (wrong catalog name, missing secret scope, different cluster policy) without a live deployment.

options.text: Run OPTIMIZE on the production output Delta tables to check if their schema matches staging
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE is a data maintenance operation; running it on output tables reveals nothing about why a deployment or job configuration failed.

options.text: Compare the job run error message in the production job run against the staging run history to identify the first diverging step
options.explanation: Comparing the production error message with staging run history pinpoints the exact task, step, or configuration value that behaves differently between environments, narrowing the search to a specific failure cause.

options.text: Enable liquid clustering on the production cluster to improve state management
options.explanation: Liquid clustering affects data file layout on tables; it has no relationship to job or bundle deployment configuration differences between environments.

questions.stem: A gold table storing daily sales aggregates is rebuilt in full every night using a complete overwrite. Which Delta Lake write pattern ensures the old data is atomically replaced and readers never see a partially written state?

options.text: Delete all rows with DELETE FROM, then insert new rows with INSERT INTO in two separate transactions
options.explanation: Executing DELETE then INSERT as two separate transactions creates a window between the two commits during which the table appears empty to concurrent readers — this violates the atomicity requirement.

options.text: Write the new data with mode('overwrite') using a DataFrame write, which creates a new table version atomically
options.explanation: Delta Lake's overwrite mode performs the entire operation as a single atomic transaction; the new version becomes visible only when the write completes successfully, ensuring readers never see a partial state.

options.text: Drop the table and recreate it so readers can query it immediately after the CREATE TABLE statement
options.explanation: Dropping and recreating a table is a two-step operation (DROP then CREATE) involving two separate commits; there is a window after the DROP during which the table does not exist, causing readers to fail.

options.text: Use VACUUM with RETAIN 0 HOURS immediately before the write to clear old files and make room for new data
options.explanation: VACUUM removes unreferenced data files and is a cleanup operation, not a write operation; it does not stage new data and does not participate in atomic data replacement.

questions.reference: [Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)

questions.stem: A team is designing a fact table for a high-volume event stream. Select TWO design decisions that BEST balance query performance and write scalability on Delta Lake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Partition the table by event_date so partition pruning eliminates entire days for date-range queries
options.explanation: Partitioning a high-volume event table by a low-cardinality column like event_date (365 values per year) creates a manageable number of partitions while enabling very effective partition pruning for date-range queries, which are typical for event analytics.

options.text: Partition the table by event_id (UUID) to maximize write parallelism
options.explanation: Partitioning by UUID event_id creates as many partitions as there are events (potentially billions), which creates extreme small-file problems and listing overhead — this is a classic over-partitioning anti-pattern.

options.text: Apply liquid clustering on the most common filter columns rather than static date partitioning
options.explanation: Liquid clustering adapts to evolving query patterns without requiring static partition boundaries; it co-locates rows with similar clustering key values across files, providing data skipping for multi-dimensional filters.

options.text: Use OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY the most selective filter columns to co-locate frequently co-queried values
options.explanation: Actually, D is also a valid technique (ZORDER is an effective data skipping strategy) but the question asks for TWO from A, B, C, D. Between the four options, A and C are the BEST pair: A for date-based pruning which is standard practice, and C for flexible multi-dimensional skipping. D is valid but somewhat superseded by liquid clustering (C) on modern Databricks.

questions.stem: A senior engineer reviews a Databricks job that reads from three silver tables using Structured Streaming and joins them using stateful stream-stream joins with watermarks. The job has been running for 6 months and state store size is growing unboundedly. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The streaming job is not configured with a checkpoint and accumulates state in driver memory
options.explanation: The job has been running for 6 months, implying checkpoints are working (the job would have failed on restart without one); the state store is on disk, not purely in driver memory.

options.text: The watermark is not defined on all input streams in the join, preventing state from being evicted when events age out
options.explanation: For stream-stream joins to evict state, Structured Streaming requires a watermark on both (all) input streams; if any stream in the join lacks a watermark, the engine cannot determine when it is safe to evict old join-candidate rows, causing the state store to grow without bound as events accumulate.

options.text: OPTIMIZE is not running on the silver tables, so file accumulation is being counted as state store growth
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE compacts Delta data files on disk; the Structured Streaming state store is a separate RocksDB-backed store and its size is entirely independent of the Delta table file count.

options.text: Delta Sharing is enabled on the silver tables, causing the join state to be replicated to external recipients
options.explanation: Delta Sharing is an outbound data sharing protocol; it does not interact with or replicate internal Spark streaming state stores.
